retrieved_docs.title: Abdominal Angina(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory tests are not helpful in the diagnosis. However, one must always rule out malignancy of the colon and stomach. Ultrasound is not sensitive to make a diagnosis of mesenteric ischemia. The gold standard is an angiogram. No laboratory test is diagnostic. A mesenteric angiogram is the standard diagnostic study. CT angiography has become the test of choice and as sensitive as an angiogram. Since these patients have atherosclerotic disease, one should work up the patient for heart and peripheral vascular disease. If they are heavy smokers, arterial blood gas and pulmonary function tests should be ordered if the patient is being worked up for surgery. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Case Study: 32-Year-Old Male Presenting with Right Lower Quadrant Abdominal Pain (Archived) -- Case Presentation
retrieved_docs.content: The patient denies prior abdominal surgeries. He does smoke a half pack of cigarettes a day, drinks alcohol socially, and denies any recreational drug use. He notes no inherited medical conditions in his family. Vital signs on arrival: Blood pressure (BP) 120/73 Heart rate (HR) 60 RR 18 Temperature: 35.6 C Oxygen saturation: 98% on room air Height 5'10" (178 cm) Weight 154 lbs (70 kg)

retrieved_docs.title: Alcoholic Pancreatitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical features include constant upper abdominal pain, with or without radiation to the back, and tenderness of the abdomen with palpation. Laboratory criteria include a serum lipase of at least three times the upper limit of normal.  Lipase peaks more rapidly, remains elevated for a more extended period, and has a higher sensitivity and specificity than amylase. Testing for both enzymes does not improve sensitivity or specificity.  A hepatic panel, calcium level, and triglyceride level may help differentiate alcoholic from other causes of AP. Finally, computerized tomography (CT) of abdomen with IV contrast is the imaging modality of choice and findings may include pancreatic enlargement, loss of pancreatic borders, surrounding fluid, and/or fat stranding. Areas of no enhancement indicate pancreatic necrosis. Imaging is normal in 15% to 30% of cases of mild pancreatitis and is not routinely indicated or needed for diagnosis. An exception to this rule is in cases where the patient does not respond to treatment after 48 hours, and further imaging can rule out other abdominal pathology or complications. Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) is not beneficial in alcohol-induced pancreatitis unless there are signs of comorbid biliary etiology [10] .

retrieved_docs.title: Abdominal Angina(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic pancreatitis Intra abdominal malinancy Chronic cholecystitis Peptic ulcer disease

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Abdomen -- Evaluation -- Epigastric Pain
retrieved_docs.content: The initial evaluation of epigastric pain is the same as that of right upper quadrant pain. Patients with cardiac risk factors and symptoms suggestive of a cardiac issue—such as dyspnea, exertional symptoms, diaphoresis, nausea, or vomiting—should undergo an electrocardiogram, troponin testing, and a chest radiograph. Based on the results, further evaluations may include echocardiography, stress testing, coronary CT angiography, and angiography. Endoscopy may be indicated for patients with suspected peptic ulcer disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Alcoholic Ketoacidosis(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal pain is commonly present, although it may be secondary to alcoholic gastritis or pancreatitis. Rebound tenderness is uncommon, and the pain is typically epigastric in nature. If rebound tenderness or peritoneal signs are present, another cause of the pain should be sought.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Abdomen -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: Past medical history, including previous surgeries, alcohol use disorder, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease, to assess for the risk of bowel obstruction due to adhesions, myocardial infarction, pancreatitis, gastritis, abdominal aortic aneurysm, and liver dysfunction

retrieved_docs.title: Alcoholic Pancreatitis -- History and Physical -- Examination
retrieved_docs.content: May be normal Guarding Tenderness with peritonism Jaundice In severe cases, alcohol-induced pancreatitis may be classically associated with periumbilical or flank ecchymosis, also known as Cullen’s or Grey Turner’s signs respectively, and represent significant peritoneal or retroperitoneal hemorrhage. Severe presentations of AP may include peritonitis, sepsis, acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), and/or shock.

retrieved_docs.title: Abdominal Angina(Archived) -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The typical clinical presentation of abdominal angina is severe pain out of proportion to the physical exam. The patient usually complains of severe abdominal pain that starts after eating a meal. In most cases, the pain gradually subsides after a few hours. Because the pain is so severe, most people develop a fear of eating and hence lose weight. One should always ask about a history of smoking, claudication, and hyperlipidemia. On physical exam, marked weight loss is obvious. The abdomen is often soft, and there are no peritoneal signs. An abdominal bruit may be heard in some patients. One may also find features of peripheral vascular disease with diminished pulses in the leg. The digital rectal exam is usually guaiac negative.

retrieved_docs.title: Boerhaave Syndrome -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Boerhaave syndrome is rare, with no predictive models. Patients with known excessive use of alcohol and binge eating should be educated regarding the pathology. They should be counseled that any sudden chest, neck, or abdominal pain with forceful emesis while indulging should be evaluated immediately. Those with Boerhaave syndrome merit close follow-up with gastroenterology and their primary care provider.

retrieved_docs.title: Alcoholic Pancreatitis -- History and Physical -- Symptoms
retrieved_docs.content: Acute abdominal pain. Typically epigastric that radiates to the back. Of note, pain may be less abrupt and poorly localized in alcoholic pancreatitis versus gallstone-induced AP. Nausea or vomiting Anorexia Chronic pancreatitis may display the additional features of pancreatic insufficiency, including steatorrhea secondary to fat malabsorption and pancreatic diabetes secondary to endocrine cell destruction.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Coronary Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: In the physical exam, general distress and diaphoresis are often seen. Heart sounds are frequently normal. At times, gallop and murmur can be heard. Lung exam is normal, although at times crackles may be heard pointing toward associated congestive heart failure (CHF). Bilateral leg edema may be present indicating CHF. The rest of the systems are typically within normal limits unless co-pathologies are present. The presence of abdominal tenderness to palpation should make the provider consider other pathologies like pancreatitis and gastritis. The presence of unequal pulses warrants consideration of aortic dissection. The presence of unilateral leg swelling should warrant work-up for pulmonary emboli. Hence a thorough physical exam is very important to rule out other life-threatening differentials.

retrieved_docs.title: Alcoholic Pancreatitis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnoses include, but are not limited to: Pancreatitis secondary to other causes Gastritis or gastroenteritis Biliary disease secondary to obstructive or infectious pathology Bowel perforation or obstruction Nephrolithiasis Ectopic pregnancy Ischemic heart disease Pneumonia Aortic pathology

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1803
retrieved_docs.content: الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية are clinically associated with تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ and with تعاطي الكحول. The presence of an abnormal chest X‑ray may therefore suggest underlying alcohol‑related pulmonary pathology, such as alcoholic emphysema, and should prompt clinicians to evaluate the patient’s history of alcohol consumption. Recognizing these associations aids in accurate diagnosis and targeted management of patients with suspected alcohol‑related lung disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1993
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal chest X‑ray findings (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) are clinically associated with alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ). In patients with alcoholic cirrhosis, chest radiographs frequently show atypical patterns that may reflect complications such as pulmonary edema, infections, or pleural effusions, underscoring the importance of evaluating chest imaging when managing this liver disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2740
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal pain (ألم بطني) is clinically associated with cocaine use (تعاطي الكوكائين), indicating that patients who consume cocaine may present with abdominal discomfort as a manifestation of the drug’s systemic effects. Recognizing this relationship is important for clinicians when evaluating unexplained abdominal pain, as it prompts consideration of substance use history and guides appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2868
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal chest X‑ray findings (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) are clinically associated with alcohol consumption (تعاطي الكحول). In patients who consume alcohol, clinicians should consider that pulmonary imaging may reveal atypical or pathological changes, which can stem from alcohol‑related lung injury, aspiration events, infections, or other respiratory complications. Recognizing this association prompts healthcare providers to inquire about alcohol use when interpreting abnormal chest radiographs, to assess for potential alcohol‑related pulmonary disease, and to incorporate appropriate counseling or treatment for alcohol misuse into the overall patient care plan.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2897
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal findings on the chest X‑ray (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) are clinically associated with alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ). In turn, alcoholic cirrhosis encompasses hepatic steatosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِري) as a related hepatic manifestation. This relationship underscores that radiographic abnormalities of the thorax can reflect underlying alcohol‑related liver disease, and that the progression of alcoholic liver injury often includes fatty infiltration of the liver (hepatic steatosis) before or alongside the development of cirrhosis. Recognizing these connections aids clinicians in linking chest imaging findings to potential liver pathology and in monitoring the spectrum of alcohol‑induced hepatic damage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2899

The ct scan abdomen is clinically associated with تصوير البطن بالموجات فوق الصوتية (abdominal ultrasound imaging). Both imaging modalities are employed to evaluate abdominal pathology, and their clinical association reflects complementary roles in the diagnostic work‑up.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2581
retrieved_docs.content: SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر (chest X‑ray) and also with gastritis and duodenitis. Gastritis and duodenitis, in turn, includes الْتِهابُ المَعِدَةِ التَّآكُلِيّ as a subordinate condition, indicating that the symptom complex may be linked both to radiographic chest findings and to inflammatory gastrointestinal disorders such as peptic ulcer disease. This interconnectedness highlights the importance of evaluating both thoracic imaging and gastrointestinal pathology when assessing patients presenting with this sign‑symptom complex.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2913
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure referred to as ct scan abdomen is clinically associated with تصوير البطن بالموجات فوق الصوتية (abdominal ultrasound imaging). In addition, ct scan abdomen is derived from the broader examination named Computerized tomography of chest and abdomen with intravenous contrast medium, indicating that abdominal CT imaging is linked both to ultrasound studies for clinical correlation and to contrast‑enhanced CT scans that encompass the chest and abdomen.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2835
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain is clinically associated with القلق (anxiety), indicating that anxiety often underlies or contributes to this vague thoracic discomfort. In addition, nonspecific chest pain shares symptom similarity with ألم بطني (abdominal pain), suggesting that patients may experience overlapping or confusing pain sensations between the chest and the abdomen. Recognizing both the psychological link to anxiety and the potential overlap with abdominal pain is important for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2842
retrieved_docs.content: Nonspecific chest pain is clinically associated with both القلق (anxiety) and with الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية (abnormal chest X‑ray). Anxiety can amplify the perception of chest discomfort, while an abnormal chest X‑ray may reveal underlying pulmonary or cardiac pathology that also manifests as nonspecific chest pain. Recognizing that nonspecific chest pain may reflect both a psychological factor (القلق) and a radiographic abnormality (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) is essential for a comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2411

This relationship guides clinicians to employ chest radiography when the SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is present, facilitating the evaluation of underlying thoracic pathology and informing subsequent patient care decisions.
